subject: Surgery
question: A 36-year-old woman has a 2.2-cm thyroid nodule and a suspicious ipsilateral cervical node. Thyroid-stimulating hormone is normal, so radionuclide scanning is not used to dismiss malignancy in this non-hyperfunctioning lesion. Ultrasound risk features prompt fine-needle aspiration of both the thyroid nodule and the node. Medullary, follicular and papillary malignancies require different biochemical and operative planning. An unlabeled cytology slide is shown; which diagnosis is demonstrated?
explanation: The slide shows papillary-type nuclear enlargement, grooves, overlapping and intranuclear cytoplasmic inclusions, supporting papillary thyroid carcinoma. Follicular adenoma cannot be distinguished from carcinoma by cytology because capsular or vascular invasion is required. Medullary carcinoma has neuroendocrine cells and amyloid, while anaplastic carcinoma is markedly pleomorphic.

images.caption: Papillary Carcinoma of the Thyroid.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Ed Uthman, MD · CC BY-SA 2.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 40-year-old woman has a thyroid malignancy and a biopsy-proven metastatic lateral cervical node from the same primary. Vocal-cord mobility is documented before surgery, calcium status is checked and imaging excludes unresectable invasion or distant metastasis. The tumour lineage has not been stated in the clinical description, yet it determines whether postoperative radioiodine can have a disease-specific adjunctive role. Simple nodule excision would leave the primary and macroscopic regional disease untreated. An unlabeled cytology image is shown; which combined treatment statement best fits the visual diagnosis?
options: Total thyroidectomy with compartment-oriented nodal dissection for medullary carcinoma, without radioiodine
Molecularly guided multimodal treatment and airway planning for anaplastic carcinoma
Core-biopsy confirmation followed primarily by systemic therapy for thyroid lymphoma
Total thyroidectomy with therapeutic neck dissection, then risk-based consideration of radioiodine
correctAnswer: Total thyroidectomy with therapeutic neck dissection, then risk-based consideration of radioiodine
explanation: The cytology is papillary thyroid carcinoma. Proven lateral nodal metastasis requires total thyroidectomy and therapeutic compartment-oriented dissection of involved nodal levels; postoperative radioiodine is then considered according to recurrence risk, residual disease and iodine avidity. Radioiodine does not replace resection of operable macroscopic disease, and calcitonin-directed pathways apply to medullary carcinoma.

images.caption: Papillary Carcinoma of the Thyroid.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Ed Uthman, MD · CC BY-SA 2.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 71-year-old man has undergone margin-controlled excision of a cutaneous malignancy. Histology and margins are complete, but the reconstructive note withholds the exact facial subunits, depth and defect size because these must be read from the displayed postoperative photograph. Primary closure, a full-thickness graft, a local transposition flap and a staged interpolated flap have different risks of alar distortion. The examination image is unlabeled and no reconstructive plan is supplied. Which reconstructive principle is most appropriate for the photographed defect?
options: Restore alar lining, support and cover with a subunit-based local or interpolated flap as required
Close the defect under maximal transverse tension with no cartilage support
Allow every full-thickness alar defect to granulate without reconstruction
Use a split-thickness graft alone despite exposed cartilage and rim instability
correctAnswer: Restore alar lining, support and cover with a subunit-based local or interpolated flap as required
explanation: The photograph shows a multi-subunit nasal-ala defect in which reconstruction must preserve the airway and alar contour by replacing any missing lining, structural cartilage support and skin cover. A subunit-based local or interpolated flap, often with cartilage grafting when support is deficient, avoids alar notching and collapse. Tension closure, secondary intention for every defect and an unsupported thin graft are inappropriate for this geometry.

images.caption: Repair of a multi-subunit defect of the nasal ala, perialar cheek, and apical triangle of the upper lip. - Post-Mohs surgical defect, left nasal ala, extending onto the left perialar cheek and apical triangle of the upper lip, 1.5 × 1.5 cm.
images.credit: Image credit: Le M, Nanda R, Khanna M. · CC BY

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient has the post-Mohs nasal defect shown in the unlabeled clinical photograph. The oncologic margin is clear, but the reconstructive team is concerned that a superficially acceptable closure could later produce external-valve collapse, alar retraction and nostril asymmetry. The exact subunits and loss of support are not narrated and must be judged from the photograph. A flat low-risk cheek defect would not require the same structural reconstruction. Which added measure most directly prevents the principal late functional deformity?
options: Cartilage batten or rim support incorporated into the reconstruction
Repeated curettage of the wound base
Immediate radiotherapy to contract the defect
correctAnswer: Cartilage batten or rim support incorporated into the reconstruction
explanation: A defect involving the nasal ala can lose the fibrous and cartilaginous support of the external nasal valve. A cartilage batten or alar-rim graft resists inspiratory collapse, retraction and notching while soft-tissue coverage restores the subunit. Purse-string tension can worsen distortion, curettage is not reconstruction and radiotherapy does not replace structural support.

images.caption: Repair of a multi-subunit defect of the nasal ala, perialar cheek, and apical triangle of the upper lip. - Post-Mohs surgical defect, left nasal ala, extending onto the left perialar cheek and apical triangle of the upper lip, 1.5 × 1.5 cm.
images.credit: Image credit: Le M, Nanda R, Khanna M. · CC BY

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 32-year-old woman with an autoimmune background develops painful lower-leg ulcers after minor trauma. Bacterial cultures are non-diagnostic, arterial pulses are present and venous duplex does not show reflux severe enough to explain the lesions. Infection, vasculitic ulceration, venous disease and a neutrophilic dermatosis remain plausible because no single laboratory test is diagnostic. A four-panel clinical figure containing baseline and follow-up photographs is displayed without its diagnostic caption, and the ulcer edge plus evolution must be assessed directly. Which diagnosis best matches the visual-clinical pattern?
explanation: The published photograph shows an active painful lower-leg ulcer with an inflammatory undermined edge in the setting of autoimmune disease, supporting pyoderma gangrenosum after infection and vascular mimics are excluded. Venous ulcers usually accompany venous hypertension, ecthyma gangrenosum occurs in severe infection or immunosuppression and neuropathic ulcers arise at pressure points with sensory loss. No single biopsy feature is pathognomonic.

images.caption: PMC12963896 - Clinical improvement of patient 1 with bimekizumab therapy. A, B, Baseline clinical images of the right pretibial shin and lateral leg demonstrate violaceous, undermined ulceration
images.credit: Image credit: NCBI · CC BY

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient has the lower-leg ulcers shown in the baseline panels of the displayed clinical figure. Infection, arterial insufficiency and venous disease have been reasonably excluded, but an invasive procedure has not yet been selected. Continued serial excision, compression alone, antibacterial treatment and immunosuppression could lead to very different outcomes. The written stem withholds the diagnostic morphology and later response, which must be read from the image panels. Which management is most appropriate?
options: Perform repeated wide debridement until the entire inflammatory edge is removed
Treat only with high-compression bandaging for venous hypertension
Use prolonged antibacterial therapy as definitive treatment despite negative infection work-up
Avoid further traumatic debridement and start specialist-directed anti-inflammatory or immunosuppressive therapy
correctAnswer: Avoid further traumatic debridement and start specialist-directed anti-inflammatory or immunosuppressive therapy
explanation: Pyoderma gangrenosum can worsen after trauma through pathergy, so repeated aggressive debridement should be avoided unless true necrotizing infection coexists. Wound care, pain control and specialist-directed corticosteroid, ciclosporin or biologic therapy are chosen according to severity and comorbidity. Compression and antibiotics may address selected adjunctive problems but are not definitive treatment for the neutrophilic disease.

images.caption: PMC12963896 - Clinical improvement of patient 1 with bimekizumab therapy. A, B, Baseline clinical images of the right pretibial shin and lateral leg demonstrate violaceous, undermined ulceration
images.credit: Image credit: NCBI · CC BY

subject: Surgery
question: A 70-kg adult is transferred after a flame injury sustained in an enclosed room. Airway inspection and carboxyhaemoglobin assessment are completed before attention turns to fluid resuscitation. Only partial- and full-thickness injury is present over the entire body regions colored pink and green on the displayed adult chart; the orange, purple and yellow regions are uninjured. Simple erythema elsewhere is excluded from the calculation. Using the percentages assigned to those color-coded regions in the image, what total body-surface area is burned?
explanation: The displayed chart colors both upper limbs pink and assigns 9% to each; it colors the head and neck green and assigns another 9%. Complete injury to those color-coded regions is therefore 9 + 9 + 9 = 27% TBSA. Only partial- and full-thickness burns count toward resuscitation TBSA; simple erythema does not.

images.caption: 513 Degree of burns.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: OpenStax College · CC BY 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 70-kg adult reaches the burns unit two hours after injury and has no evidence of heart failure or chronic kidney disease. The airway has been secured, carbon-monoxide toxicity is treated and two large peripheral cannulas are functioning. Partial- and full-thickness injury covers every region colored pink and green on the displayed adult chart image, with all other color-coded regions spared. Lactated Ringer solution is selected, and the initial calculation will subsequently be titrated to urine output and perfusion. Using the American Burn Association adult starting estimate of 2 mL/kg/%TBSA for a burn of at least 20% TBSA, how much crystalloid is calculated for the first twenty-four hours?
explanation: The image corresponds to 27% TBSA, so the current ABA-recommended adult starting estimate is 2 mL x 70 kg x 27 = 3,780 mL in the first twenty-four hours from the time of burn. This lower starting calculation is intended to reduce fluid creep; the actual rate is repeatedly titrated to urine output and perfusion rather than treated as a fixed prescription. The traditional 4-mL Parkland calculation would yield 7,560 mL but is not the contemporary ABA starting recommendation for this adult.

images.caption: 513 Degree of burns.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: OpenStax College · CC BY 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 31-year-old man has pleuritic pain and dyspnoea after blunt chest trauma. Blood pressure is stable, the trachea is central and there is no tension physiology. Pleural air, pleural blood, pulmonary contusion and lobar collapse remain possible explanations for asymmetric ventilation, while subcutaneous emphysema limits bedside ultrasound. A two-panel figure contains the initial chest radiograph and a post-intervention film, but the decisive signs are not narrated in the stem. Which principal diagnosis is demonstrated on the initial radiograph?
explanation: Panel a shows a visible right visceral pleural line with absent peripheral lung markings and substantial separation from the chest wall, establishing a large right pneumothorax. Haemothorax creates dependent fluid opacity, lobar collapse produces volume loss and contusion produces parenchymal air-space opacity. Panel b demonstrates re-expansion after pleural decompression.

subject: Surgery
question: A second trauma patient has the same initial pattern on the displayed panel-a radiograph and will require prolonged positive-pressure ventilation for associated injuries. Haemodynamics are stable, the trachea is central and there is no current tension physiology. The written stem does not name the pleural or parenchymal abnormality; it must be identified from the image before recurrence risk can be judged. Supplemental oxygen is running, and no thoracotomy indication is present. Which pleural intervention is safest before prolonged ventilation?
options: Routine observation without a pleural-drain plan
explanation: Panel a establishes a large traumatic right pneumothorax. With planned prolonged positive-pressure ventilation, tube thoracostomy provides durable evacuation and monitoring; aspiration alone is less secure in this context. Selected small occult pneumothoraces may be observed with close monitoring, needle decompression is reserved for tension physiology and thoracotomy is not required for isolated pleural air.

subject: Surgery
question: A 56-year-old man with diabetes develops severe leg pain and systemic illness after a minor skin wound. Temperature is 38.1 degrees Celsius, lactate is initially normal and there is no palpable crepitus, so cellulitis, pyomyositis, venous occlusion and a deep necrotizing infection remain plausible before the skin is inspected. Broad-spectrum antibiotics and resuscitation begin while operative help is summoned. An unlabeled preoperative clinical photograph is displayed, and the candidate must assess the skin colour, bullae, epidermis and necrosis directly. Which diagnosis is most consistent with the visual findings?
explanation: The photograph shows extensive skin necrosis, bullae, ecchymosis and epidermal separation in a toxic patient, strongly supporting necrotizing fasciitis. Uncomplicated cellulitis does not usually produce widespread necrosis or bullae, while pyomyositis is centred in muscle and venous thrombosis does not explain this destructive skin pattern. Imaging must not delay exploration when suspicion is high.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with diabetes has progressive limb pain and fever despite initial treatment for presumed cellulitis. Haemodynamics are temporarily stable, while cellulitis, pyomyositis and a necrotizing infection remain competing pathways. Broad aerobic-anaerobic antimicrobial coverage and fluid resuscitation are already being administered. An unlabeled preoperative photograph is displayed, and the extent plus depth implied by its skin findings must be assessed without a narrated caption. Which intervention best matches the visual diagnosis?
options: Continue intravenous antibiotics for uncomplicated cellulitis
Radical surgical debridement to viable tissue
Image-guided drainage of a localized intramuscular abscess
Therapeutic anticoagulation for acute deep venous thrombosis
correctAnswer: Radical surgical debridement to viable tissue
explanation: Necrotizing soft-tissue infection requires immediate wide exploration and excision of all non-viable tissue, often with planned re-exploration, alongside antibiotics and organ support. Antibiotics cannot penetrate thrombosed necrotic fascia adequately. Aspiration or dressings provide neither diagnosis nor source control.

subject: Surgery
question: A newborn has difficulty maintaining a feeding seal but breathes comfortably without cyanotic episodes. Weight, hearing screen and cardiac examination are recorded, and parents receive feeding support before discharge. An incomplete unilateral cleft, a complete unilateral cleft, a bilateral cleft and a median facial cleft require different markings and reconstructive plans. The written note deliberately does not specify whether the defect reaches the nostril floor. A preoperative frontal clinical photograph is shown; which anomaly is visually demonstrated?
explanation: The frontal photograph shows a left-sided cleft extending through the full lip height into the nostril floor with characteristic alar distortion, defining a complete unilateral cleft lip. An incomplete cleft stops short of the nostril floor, bilateral disease affects both sides and a median cleft lies in the midline. The photograph does not expose the palate, so palatal involvement is not asserted from this image.

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: An infant with the photographed craniofacial defect is gaining weight with specialised feeding and has no active respiratory infection. Anaesthetic assessment confirms that the airway can be managed safely, while the palate and hearing require separate direct examination because they are not visible in the frontal image. Incomplete unilateral, complete unilateral, bilateral and median lip defects require different markings and reconstructive plans. The exact lip anatomy is withheld from the written stem and must be classified from the photograph. Which operative plan best matches it?
options: Bilateral cleft-lip repair with premaxillary setback as a routine first step
Palatoplasty alone with no lip repair
Observation until adolescence before primary lip repair
Early-infant unilateral cleft-lip repair using rotation-advancement or an equivalent anatomic technique
correctAnswer: Early-infant unilateral cleft-lip repair using rotation-advancement or an equivalent anatomic technique
explanation: A complete unilateral cleft lip is repaired in early infancy once growth and anaesthetic fitness are adequate, using a rotation-advancement or other accepted anatomic technique to reconstruct muscle, lip landmarks and nasal base. Bilateral protocols address different anatomy, palatoplasty alone leaves the lip unrepaired and delaying primary lip repair to adolescence is inappropriate. Palatal management is planned separately after direct assessment.

images.caption: PMC13250337 - Preoperative frontal view showing a left complete unilateral cleft lip.
images.credit: Image credit: NCBI · CC BY

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 42-year-old woman has episodic headache, sweating, palpitations and labile hypertension. Plasma free metanephrines are repeatedly elevated after interfering drugs and acute illness are excluded. A catecholamine-secreting adrenal tumour, extra-adrenal paraganglioma and multifocal hereditary disease require different operative exposure and genetic evaluation. Cross-sectional imaging is indeterminate because several small retroperitoneal nodules are present. An unlabeled functional nuclear image is shown; which lesion pattern is demonstrated?
explanation: The functional image localises avid uptake to one adrenal gland without extra-adrenal or contralateral disease, supporting a solitary adrenal pheochromocytoma. Biochemistry establishes catecholamine excess but cannot determine location or multiplicity. Extra-adrenal uptake would alter the surgical field and genetic implications.

images.caption: Pheochromocytoma Scan.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Drahreg01 · CC BY-SA 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with biochemically proven catecholamine excess has no metastatic disease and acceptable cardiopulmonary reserve. Alpha blockade and volume expansion are completed, with beta blockade added only afterward for persistent tachycardia. Cross-sectional imaging shows several indeterminate retroperitoneal nodules, so the operative field and need for cortical preservation cannot be chosen from that study alone. An unlabeled functional image is shown, and the location plus multiplicity of uptake must determine the operation. Which procedure best matches the visual localization?
options: Open excision of an organ-of-Zuckerkandl paraganglioma
Bilateral total adrenalectomy with lifelong steroid replacement
Percutaneous biopsy of the avid lesion without resection
explanation: The functional image shows a solitary adrenal focus without contralateral or extra-adrenal uptake, so unilateral adrenalectomy is appropriate after biochemical preparation; a minimally invasive approach is usual when size and local anatomy permit. Extra-adrenal uptake would require paraganglioma-directed exposure, while bilateral disease may prompt a cortical-sparing discussion in selected hereditary cases. Percutaneous biopsy of a catecholamine-secreting lesion is hazardous and unnecessary.

images.caption: Pheochromocytoma Scan.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Drahreg01 · CC BY-SA 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 61-year-old man has intermittent painless haematuria and modest unintentional weight loss. Renal function is preserved, urine culture is negative and cystoscopy does not show a bladder source. An upper-tract urothelial tumour, angiomyolipoma, complex cyst and renal cortical malignancy remain possible before lesion composition and enhancement are assessed. Chest staging will be arranged if the lesion proves malignant because pulmonary spread would change intent. Unlabeled axial and coronal multiphasic CT panels are displayed; which diagnosis best fits the visual pattern?
explanation: The CT shows a large enhancing mass centred in the renal cortex, supporting renal cell carcinoma. Urothelial carcinoma is centred in the collecting system, angiomyolipoma usually contains macroscopic fat and a simple cyst does not enhance. The source does not clearly demonstrate renal-vein thrombus, so venous extension is not asserted.

images.caption: Corticomedullary phase CT in axial and coronal plane, and parenchymal phase, of renal cell carcinoma.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Kristie Guite, Louis Hinshaw and Fred Lee · CC BY 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A fit 59-year-old man has the renal lesion shown on the unlabeled axial and coronal CT panels, with no distant metastasis. Renal function in the opposite kidney is normal, and cardiopulmonary assessment supports major surgery. A small peripheral cortical tumour, an upper-tract urothelial lesion, a frail-patient ablation candidate and a large organ-confined cortical malignancy require different strategies. Needle biopsy is reserved for a result that would alter management, and no venous thrombus is claimed from this source. After assessing size, position and enhancement on the image, which operation is most appropriate?
options: Partial nephrectomy for a small peripheral T1a lesion
Radical nephrectomy for a large localized renal cortical mass
correctAnswer: Radical nephrectomy for a large localized renal cortical mass
explanation: The image shows a large centrally involving renal cortical mass in a fit patient with a normal contralateral kidney, making radical nephrectomy appropriate when nephron-sparing resection is not technically or oncologically suitable. Nephroureterectomy treats urothelial carcinoma, while ablation and surveillance are generally used for selected small masses or frail patients. The operation does not include unsupported tumour-thrombectomy planning.

images.caption: Corticomedullary phase CT in axial and coronal plane, and parenchymal phase, of renal cell carcinoma.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Kristie Guite, Louis Hinshaw and Fred Lee · CC BY 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A blast victim has facial burns, singed nasal hair, soot in the mouth and progressive hoarseness. Oxygen saturation is initially acceptable, but airway oedema may worsen rapidly after fluid resuscitation and make later intubation impossible. Carbon monoxide and lower-airway injury are assessed in parallel. What early priority is most important?
options: Controlled endotracheal intubation before upper-airway access is lost
High-flow oxygen with serial observation until obstruction develops
Diagnostic bronchoscopy before securing the threatened upper airway
Emergency cricothyrotomy while oral intubation remains readily feasible
correctAnswer: Controlled endotracheal intubation before upper-airway access is lost
explanation: Facial burns with soot and progressive hoarseness demand controlled endotracheal intubation before oedema makes the airway inaccessible. High-flow oxygen and evaluation for carbon monoxide accompany resuscitation, while bronchoscopy evaluates lower-airway injury after the threatened upper airway is secured; surgical airway is reserved for failed conventional access.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: After resection of a lateral-tongue squamous-cell carcinoma, final pathology shows microscopic invasive carcinoma at the deep margin. There is no extranodal extension, restaging shows no distant disease and a further deep resection is technically feasible without prohibitive functional loss. The multidisciplinary team wants to correct the dominant local-risk factor before finalising adjuvant treatment. In this setting, which next step is preferred?
options: Postoperative radiotherapy accepting the involved deep margin
Surgical re-excision to obtain a clear deep margin
Postoperative chemoradiotherapy accepting the involved deep margin
Close surveillance accepting the involved deep margin
correctAnswer: Surgical re-excision to obtain a clear deep margin
explanation: A microscopically involved oral-cavity margin is a major local recurrence risk. When further resection is technically feasible with acceptable morbidity, re-resection to achieve a clear margin is preferred before adjuvant treatment is finalised. Postoperative chemoradiotherapy is used when a positive margin cannot be cleared or when other major high-risk features remain, but it is not a substitute for achievable margin clearance. Surveillance alone is inappropriate.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient has recurrent perianal discharge from an external opening located posterior to the transverse anal line. MRI is planned because previous sepsis may have created secondary extensions. The examiner first applies Goodsall rule to predict the likely route toward the internal opening before definitive sphincter-preserving treatment. How does a posterior opening usually track?
options: Along a straight radial tract to the nearest crypt
Superiorly to the umbilicus through a urachal remnant
Along a curved tract to the posterior midline
Anteriorly into the urethra without crossing sphincter
correctAnswer: Along a curved tract to the posterior midline
explanation: Goodsall rule predicts that posterior external openings usually curve to a posterior midline internal opening, whereas many anterior openings close to the anus run radially. The rule has exceptions, especially long anterior tracts and complex disease, so MRI or examination under anaesthesia defines anatomy before surgery.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A haemodynamically stable trauma patient has a high-grade splenic injury on CT with focal arterial contrast blush but no peritonitis. Serial haemoglobin suggests ongoing bleeding. The trauma centre wants to preserve splenic tissue while controlling the arterial source without laparotomy. Which minimally invasive adjunct is appropriate?
options: Use monitored non-operative management with interval CT alone
Use operative splenorrhaphy with topical haemostatic agents
Use operative splenectomy with postsplenectomy vaccination
Use selective angiography with splenic-artery embolisation
correctAnswer: Use selective angiography with splenic-artery embolisation
explanation: In a stable patient, selective angiography and embolisation can control arterial bleeding and improve splenic salvage when CT shows contrast blush, pseudoaneurysm or other high-risk features. Observation alone is less suitable with active arterial extravasation and falling haemoglobin. Splenorrhaphy or splenectomy is reserved for patients requiring laparotomy because of instability, peritonitis or failed non-operative management.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: After alkali ingestion, endoscopy shows deep circumferential oesophageal injury and the patient cannot swallow safely for several weeks. Airway and perforation have been addressed, and the nutrition team wants to preserve enteral feeding while avoiding repeated passage through severely injured oesophagus. Prolonged parenteral nutrition is undesirable when the distal gut works. In this setting, which route is appropriate?
options: Surgically placed feeding jejunostomy for enteral nutrition
Endoscopically placed nasojejunal tube across the injured oesophagus
Percutaneously placed gastrostomy for long-term gastric feeding
Centrally delivered parenteral nutrition for the full recovery period
correctAnswer: Surgically placed feeding jejunostomy for enteral nutrition
explanation: A feeding jejunostomy provides durable enteral nutrition when oral and transoesophageal feeding are unsafe but the distal gut is functional. Passing a tube across a deeply injured oesophagus risks further trauma or perforation. Gastrostomy may complicate later reconstruction and does not bypass all concerns in severe corrosive injury, while prolonged parenteral nutrition is less desirable when safe enteral access can be created.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 39-year-old man treated incompletely for genitourinary tuberculosis develops severe frequency, urgency and suprapubic pain. Imaging shows upper-tract scarring, cystoscopy reveals a fibrotic low-capacity bladder and urine cultures for ordinary bacteria are repeatedly negative. Urodynamic and renal-function assessment guide reconstructive planning. In this setting, which late complication is present?
explanation: Healing genitourinary tuberculosis can produce dense bladder fibrosis and a very small thimble bladder, causing severe frequency and threatening the upper tracts. Radiation and neurogenic disease can also reduce capacity, but the tuberculosis history and associated tract scarring identify the cause.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 42-year-old road-drill operator reports episodic sharply demarcated blanching and numbness of several fingers precipitated by cold. Radial and ulnar pulses are normal, there is no lower-limb claudication and symptoms developed after years of high-frequency tool exposure. Occupational assessment also identifies reduced fingertip sensation and grip endurance. In this setting, which syndrome best integrates the vascular and neurological findings?
explanation: Hand-arm vibration syndrome combines secondary Raynaud-type digital vasospasm with sensory and musculoskeletal effects after chronic exposure to vibrating tools. Hypothenar-hammer syndrome follows repetitive ulnar-palmar trauma and usually produces ulnar-artery injury. Thromboangiitis obliterans is linked to tobacco-related distal inflammatory occlusion, while primary Raynaud phenomenon lacks the occupational neurological component.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 63-year-old man has abrupt tearing chest pain radiating to the back, pulse asymmetry and a new aortic-regurgitation murmur. CT angiogram shows an intimal flap involving the ascending aorta. Blood pressure and shear stress are controlled during immediate cardiothoracic mobilisation. Which management principle is correct?
options: Use intravenous anti-impulse therapy with interval aortic surveillance
Use thoracic endovascular stent-grafting of the descending thoracic aorta
Use catheter fenestration of the distal dissection flap with branch stenting
Use emergency surgical repair for Stanford type A dissection
correctAnswer: Use emergency surgical repair for Stanford type A dissection
explanation: Dissection involving the ascending aorta is Stanford type A and generally requires emergency open repair because of rupture, tamponade, coronary malperfusion and aortic-valve complications. Intravenous anti-impulse therapy begins immediately but is a bridge to operation. Descending-aortic stent grafting and distal fenestration are used for selected type B dissection or malperfusion anatomy and do not treat the proximal type A tear.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with chronic venous disease has oedema, lipodermatosclerosis and an open gaiter-area ulcer with granulation tissue. Duplex ultrasound maps reflux and ABI is adequate for compression. The vascular clinic records baseline severity using the clinical component of CEAP before treatment. Which class describes an active venous ulcer?
explanation: CEAP clinical class C6 denotes an active venous ulcer. C5 denotes a healed ulcer, C4b includes lipodermatosclerosis or atrophie blanche without active ulceration and C3 denotes oedema without the more advanced skin or ulcer findings.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with varicose veins has the reported brown gaiter pigmentation and venous eczema but no lipodermatosclerosis, healed ulcer or active ulcer. Duplex maps reflux for treatment. The clinician assigns the CEAP class from the current skin manifestations. Which clinical class is most appropriate?
explanation: CEAP C4a denotes pigmentation or venous eczema. C3 denotes oedema without skin change, C5 a healed venous ulcer and C6 an active ulcer, while lipodermatosclerosis or atrophie blanche would be C4b.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient has a shallow irregular medial gaiter ulcer with oedema, lipodermatosclerosis and duplex-confirmed superficial venous reflux. Pedal pulses are palpable and ankle-brachial index is 1.0, so arterial inflow is adequate. Wound care, exercise and reflux treatment are planned around the main outpatient measure. In this setting, which therapy is central?
options: Intermittent pneumatic compression for active ulcer healing
Graduated multilayer high-compression bandaging for active ulcer healing
Class-II compression hosiery for active ulcer healing
correctAnswer: Graduated multilayer high-compression bandaging for active ulcer healing
explanation: Graduated multilayer high compression is the foundation of active venous-ulcer healing when arterial supply is adequate, combined with wound care, mobility and treatment of relevant reflux. Light compression may be chosen when arterial disease limits pressure but is less effective with a normal ankle-brachial index. Intermittent pneumatic compression is generally adjunctive, and hosiery is particularly useful after healing to reduce recurrence rather than as the sole treatment for an open exudative ulcer.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: An elderly patient with atrial fibrillation has severe abdominal pain and CT evidence of acute mesenteric arterial occlusion with non-enhancing bowel. At laparotomy, one segment is frankly gangrenous while adjacent bowel is of uncertain viability, and vascular inflow may still be restored. The team plans a second look if necessary. In this setting, which operative principle is appropriate?
options: Restore mesenteric perfusion and retain the frankly gangrenous segment
Resect the frankly gangrenous segment while leaving arterial inflow occluded
Resect gangrenous and equivocal bowel during the first operation
Resect frankly non-viable bowel and restore mesenteric perfusion when feasible
correctAnswer: Resect frankly non-viable bowel and restore mesenteric perfusion when feasible
explanation: Frankly gangrenous bowel must be resected, while mesenteric revascularisation should be performed when feasible to preserve marginal intestine. Revascularisation cannot salvage established transmural gangrene, and resection alone leaves the remaining bowel at risk if arterial inflow is not restored. A planned second-look operation can reassess equivocal segments and avoid excessive initial resection.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with a shallow gaiter-area venous ulcer has adequate arterial inflow and duplex-confirmed superficial truncal reflux. Multilayer compression has begun, the patient is fit for intervention and deep venous outflow is patent. The vascular team wants to accelerate healing and reduce recurrent venous hypertension rather than wait until the ulcer has closed. In this setting, which additional strategy is preferred?
options: Continue compression and add early endovenous ablation of superficial reflux
Continue compression and defer endovenous ablation to the healed-ulcer phase
Continue compression and add deep-venous thrombolysis for superficial reflux
Continue compression and add arterial bypass despite normal inflow
correctAnswer: Continue compression and add early endovenous ablation of superficial reflux
explanation: For an active venous ulcer with suitable superficial reflux and adequate arterial inflow, early endovenous ablation added to compression accelerates healing and reduces the duration of active ulceration. Waiting until healing postpones correction of reflux. Deep-venous thrombolysis has no role without acute deep thrombosis, and arterial bypass is not indicated when inflow is normal.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient develops sudden severe leg pain, pallor, paraesthesia, weakness and absent distal pulses. The reported angiogram shows an acute femoral arterial occlusion. The limb remains potentially salvageable but is immediately threatened, so propagation must be limited while vascular imaging and operative or endovascular reperfusion are mobilised. Which treatment is central?
options: Catheter-directed thrombolysis without immediate anticoagulation or surgical backup
Systemic heparin followed by urgent operative or endovascular revascularisation
Antiplatelet therapy followed by elective outpatient arterial imaging
High-compression therapy followed by delayed venous duplex assessment
correctAnswer: Systemic heparin followed by urgent operative or endovascular revascularisation
explanation: Acute threatened limb ischaemia requires immediate unfractionated heparin unless contraindicated and urgent revascularisation by embolectomy, thrombectomy, bypass or endovascular therapy according to cause and Rutherford category. Exercise and preventive antiplatelet therapy are chronic-disease measures, not rescue treatment.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: An 82-year-old man with atrial fibrillation develops abrupt severe abdominal pain that is strikingly out of proportion to the initial examination. Lactate is rising but he is haemodynamically stable enough for imaging. The vascular and general-surgery teams need to identify arterial occlusion, bowel viability clues and possible endovascular access without delay. Which test is best?
options: D-dimer as a definitive mesenteric arterial test
CT angiography of the mesenteric circulation
Plain abdominal radiography as the sole vascular study
correctAnswer: CT angiography of the mesenteric circulation
explanation: CT angiography is the key urgent test for suspected acute mesenteric ischaemia in a stable patient because it identifies arterial or venous occlusion and assesses bowel and mesenteric changes. Laboratory tests and plain radiographs cannot exclude the diagnosis or define a revascularisation target.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A boy with posterior urethral valves presents late and undergoes successful endoscopic ablation after bladder drainage. Despite relief of the outlet obstruction, creatinine remains elevated and urodynamics show a poorly compliant high-pressure bladder. Ultrasound demonstrates dysplastic kidneys and reflux. What best explains the persistent renal dysfunction?
options: Residual valve tissue causing persistent mechanical outlet obstruction
Isolated vesicoureteric reflux causing progressive reflux nephropathy
Recurrent pyelonephritis causing newly acquired cortical scarring
Congenital renal dysplasia with chronic high-pressure bladder injury
correctAnswer: Congenital renal dysplasia with chronic high-pressure bladder injury
explanation: Posterior urethral valves can cause congenital renal dysplasia and continuing injury from a high-pressure dysfunctional bladder, reflux and infection. Successful ablation relieves the anatomical valve obstruction but cannot reverse established dysplasia or bladder remodelling. Residual valves, reflux nephropathy and recurrent pyelonephritis remain differential contributors, but the documented complete ablation, dysplastic kidneys and poor bladder compliance identify combined dysplasia and valve-bladder injury as the best explanation.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A male neonate has a poor urinary stream, palpable bladder, bilateral hydroureteronephrosis and rising creatinine. A urethral catheter passes and partially decompresses the bladder, but VCUG still shows a dilated posterior urethra and bladder-neck hypertrophy. Sepsis and electrolytes are stabilised before definitive treatment. In this setting, which statement is most accurate?
options: Perform cystoscopic valve ablation after stabilisation
Continue catheter drainage without definitive endoscopic treatment
Perform bilateral ureteric reimplantation after stabilisation
Perform repeated internal urethrotomy after stabilisation
correctAnswer: Perform cystoscopic valve ablation after stabilisation
explanation: Passing a catheter does not exclude posterior urethral valves; drainage stabilises the infant, but definitive treatment is endoscopic valve ablation when feasible. Renal dysplasia and bladder dysfunction may persist, so long-term renal, bladder and reflux surveillance remains necessary.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: An 8-year-old boy has intermittent flank pain, recurrent urinary infection and progressive unilateral hydronephrosis. Diuretic renography shows delayed drainage across the pelviureteric junction with impaired split renal function, while distal ureter and bladder are normal. Infection is controlled before reconstruction. In this setting, which operation is the standard definitive treatment?
explanation: Anderson-Hynes dismembered pyeloplasty excises the obstructed PUJ segment and re-anastomoses the spatulated ureter to the renal pelvis, providing durable drainage. Ureteroureterostomy treats selected ureteric lesions, reimplantation treats distal reflux or obstruction and valve ablation addresses posterior urethral valves.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A rapidly enlarging breast mass has leaf-like stromal architecture on core biopsy and is classified as a resectable phyllodes tumour. Imaging shows no distant disease. The surgeon plans breast conservation only if a clear margin can be obtained, while avoiding unnecessary axillary surgery because nodal spread is uncommon. What is the principal surgical treatment?
options: Simple tumour enucleation with preservation of the pseudocapsule
Wide local excision with routine axillary lymph-node dissection
Wide local excision with histologically clear margins
Simple mastectomy with routine axillary lymph-node dissection
correctAnswer: Wide local excision with histologically clear margins
explanation: A resectable phyllodes tumour requires complete excision with a negative margin, using wide local excision when breast conservation can achieve clearance and mastectomy when it cannot. Simple enucleation risks a positive margin and recurrence. Routine axillary dissection is unnecessary because phyllodes tumours spread haematogenously far more often than through lymph nodes.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A woman has rapid onset of diffuse breast erythema, oedema and peau d'orange involving more than one-third of the breast. Additional assessment demonstrates dermal lymphatic tumour emboli and no distant metastasis. The oncology team stages the clinical inflammatory phenotype before neoadjuvant systemic treatment and local therapy. Which clinical T category applies?
explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer with rapid diffuse erythema and oedema involving at least one-third of the breast is clinical T4d. Non-inflammatory ulceration, satellite nodules or skin oedema can be T4b, while T2 and T3 are primarily size-based without T4 skin or chest-wall features.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A conscious adult suddenly develops complete foreign-body airway obstruction while eating and cannot speak, cough effectively or breathe. Cyanosis is developing. The patient still has a pulse, so emergency responders begin the standard sequence while preparing to start CPR if consciousness is lost. Which action is appropriate now?
options: Give up to five back blows followed by up to five abdominal thrusts
Give up to five abdominal thrusts followed by a blind finger sweep
Give up to five chest thrusts followed by rescue breaths while conscious
Begin chest-compression cycles before the conscious patient collapses
correctAnswer: Give up to five back blows followed by up to five abdominal thrusts
explanation: Severe conscious adult choking is treated with up to five forceful back blows followed by up to five abdominal thrusts, repeated while the patient remains conscious. Chest thrusts replace abdominal thrusts in selected pregnant or markedly obese patients; CPR begins only after unresponsiveness, and blind finger sweeps are avoided.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: An unconscious trauma patient has snoring respirations after a high-speed collision. A cervical-spine injury is possible. While manual in-line stabilisation is maintained and definitive airway equipment is prepared, the first manoeuvre should open the pharynx without extending the neck. Which technique is appropriate?
options: Forceful head tilt and chin lift
Blind finger sweep of the oropharynx
Needle cricothyrotomy before attempting basic opening
explanation: A jaw thrust opens the airway while minimising cervical movement and is preferred when cervical injury is suspected, with manual stabilisation and rapid progression to a definitive airway as needed. Head tilt extends the neck, blind finger sweeps can worsen obstruction and cricothyrotomy is reserved for failed airway access.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: During massive transfusion for haemorrhagic shock, a patient develops worsening hypotension, perioral twitching and QT prolongation despite improving haemoglobin. Ionised calcium is low. The trauma team recognises that impaired hepatic clearance of the anticoagulant in blood products can compound shock and coagulopathy. Which correctable abnormality is most likely?
explanation: Citrate in transfused components binds ionised calcium, particularly during rapid high-volume transfusion, causing hypotension, myocardial dysfunction, QT prolongation and neuromuscular irritability. Ionised calcium should be monitored and replaced while hypothermia, potassium and coagulation are managed concurrently.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 24-year-old woman has a small itchy painful keloid extending beyond an earlobe-piercing wound. There is no infection or suspicion of malignancy. The plastic surgeon explains that excision alone has a high recurrence rate while pressure therapy may be an adjunct. Which common first-line treatment is appropriate?
explanation: Intralesional triamcinolone is a common first-line treatment for a symptomatic small keloid and can reduce pruritus, pain and bulk. Fluorouracil, bleomycin and verapamil are alternative or adjunct intralesional agents used selectively, often for resistant lesions or in combination protocols.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A raised pruritic scar on the reported photograph extends well beyond the margins of an ear-piercing wound and continues to enlarge months later. There is no ulceration or infection. The plastic surgeon discusses intralesional steroid, pressure therapy and the high recurrence rate after excision alone. Which diagnosis is most likely?
explanation: A keloid grows beyond the original wound boundaries and tends to persist or recur, whereas a hypertrophic scar remains within the injury and often regresses. A rapidly bleeding vascular papule or infiltrative dermal tumour has a different appearance and biopsy threshold.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A bed-bound patient has the reported sacral pressure wound with full-thickness tissue loss and directly palpable bone at its base. Slough does not obscure depth. The wound team evaluates osteomyelitis, nutrition and off-loading while documenting stage according to the deepest exposed structure. Which stage is present?
explanation: Stage 4 pressure injury exposes or makes directly palpable fascia, muscle, tendon, cartilage or bone. Stage 3 reaches adipose tissue without exposed deep supporting structures, unstageable wounds have an obscured base and deep-tissue injury has persistent deep discoloration rather than an open bone-exposing crater.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with longstanding diabetes has the reported plantar ulcer beneath a metatarsal head, surrounded by callus. The foot is warm with palpable pulses, but monofilament sensation is absent and repetitive loading has continued unnoticed. Infection and Charcot deformity are assessed separately. Which mechanism is most important?
options: Critical arterial insufficiency with absent pedal perfusion
Acute venous thrombosis causing plantar tissue necrosis
Loss of protective sensation with repetitive pressure
Immune vasculitis causing multifocal purpuric ulceration
correctAnswer: Loss of protective sensation with repetitive pressure
explanation: A callus-rimmed plantar ulcer at a pressure point with preserved warmth and pulses is typically neuropathic, caused by loss of protective sensation and repetitive mechanical loading. Off-loading is essential, while perfusion, infection and deformity still require systematic assessment.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: Six weeks after a sternotomy, a patient develops a red raised itchy scar that remains exactly within the original incision and is beginning to soften. There is no purulent discharge or recurrent tumour. The plastic-surgery team distinguishes normal exuberant healing from a keloid before choosing silicone, pressure or injection therapy. Which feature favours a hypertrophic scar?
options: It extends progressively beyond the original wound boundaries
It develops independently of any preceding skin injury
It represents a malignant dermal fibroblast proliferation
It remains confined to the original wound boundaries
correctAnswer: It remains confined to the original wound boundaries
explanation: A hypertrophic scar remains within the original wound and often regresses or softens over time, whereas a keloid extends beyond wound margins and may continue to grow. Neither lesion is malignant, and both usually follow dermal injury.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: An immobile patient has a shallow heel pressure wound with partial-thickness skin loss and exposed viable dermis. There is no visible adipose, granulation tissue, slough, eschar, fascia, tendon or bone. The wound is not merely persistent non-blanching erythema of intact skin. Which stage is present?
explanation: Stage 2 is partial-thickness skin loss with exposed dermis and no visible adipose or deeper tissue. Stage 1 has intact skin, stage 3 exposes adipose and stage 4 exposes or directly palpates fascia, muscle, tendon, cartilage or bone.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A euthyroid patient has the reported thyroid nodule with irregular margins and punctate echogenic foci. FNAC shows papillary architecture, nuclear grooves and intranuclear inclusions. A calcified concentric structure on cytology corresponds to the ultrasound microcalcification. Which diagnosis is classically associated with psammoma bodies?
explanation: Papillary thyroid carcinoma characteristically shows nuclear grooves, inclusions and psammoma bodies, which may appear as punctate echogenic foci on ultrasound. Medullary carcinoma has amyloid and calcitonin expression, while follicular carcinoma requires capsular or vascular invasion.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A child has the reported painless midline neck cyst that moves with swallowing and tongue protrusion. Ultrasound confirms a tract related to the hyoid and a normal orthotopic thyroid. The surgeon wants to minimise recurrence by removing the embryologic tract rather than shelling out the cyst alone. Which operation is indicated?
options: Simple cyst excision with division of the tract below the hyoid
Central hyoid excision while leaving the cyst and tract in situ
Sistrunk operation with tract and central hyoid excision
Suprahyoid tract excision while preserving the central hyoid segment
correctAnswer: Sistrunk operation with tract and central hyoid excision
explanation: The Sistrunk procedure removes the thyroglossal cyst, the tract through or behind the hyoid and the central hyoid segment, greatly reducing recurrence. Dividing the tract below the hyoid or preserving the central segment leaves the embryological pathway incompletely treated, while removing hyoid alone without the cyst and tract cannot cure the lesion. Acute infection is treated before definitive surgery.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A euthyroid patient has the reported thyroid nodule with marked hypoechogenicity, irregular margins and punctate echogenic foci. Serum TSH is normal, and the lesion meets size and risk criteria for tissue diagnosis rather than radionuclide scanning. Cervical nodes are surveyed at the same visit. In this setting, which diagnostic test should follow?
options: Radionuclide thyroid scan despite normal TSH
Serum thyroglobulin as a tissue diagnosis
CT angiography as the primary cytology substitute
explanation: A suspicious thyroid nodule meeting guideline thresholds is sampled by ultrasound-guided FNAC, with sonographically suspicious nodes sampled when appropriate. Radionuclide scanning is mainly used when TSH is suppressed, and serum thyroglobulin or CT cannot replace cytology for initial diagnosis.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A superficial parotid pleomorphic adenoma is mobile and apparently encapsulated. The surgeon avoids simple enucleation. Histology of these tumours can show an incomplete capsule and microscopic extensions into adjacent gland, and capsule rupture may seed multifocal recurrence that is difficult to reoperate around the facial nerve. Which feature explains the recurrence risk?
options: Microscopic pseudopods extending beyond an incomplete capsule
A uniformly complete capsule preventing microscopic extension
Early inguinal lymphatic metastasis from every primary tumour
Obligatory malignant transformation after any limited operation
correctAnswer: Microscopic pseudopods extending beyond an incomplete capsule
explanation: Pleomorphic adenoma may have an incomplete capsule, satellite nodules and microscopic pseudopods, so shelling it out can leave tumour or rupture the capsule. Formal parotidectomy with an appropriate margin reduces recurrence while preserving the facial nerve.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 19-year-old woman has a recurrent, painless, fluctuant bluish swelling on the inner lower lip after habitual lip biting. It intermittently ruptures and refills, ultrasound shows a superficial cystic lesion and examination reveals no induration, sensory loss or suspicious cervical nodes. The surgeon suspects mucus extravasation from a traumatised minor salivary gland. In this setting, which treatment offers the lowest recurrence risk?
options: Simple incision and drainage of the mucin-filled cavity
Excision of the lesion together with the feeding minor salivary glands
Needle aspiration followed by intralesional corticosteroid injection
Wide full-thickness lip excision followed by flap reconstruction
correctAnswer: Excision of the lesion together with the feeding minor salivary glands
explanation: A recurrent lower-lip mucocele is treated by excising the lesion together with adjacent feeding minor salivary glands, reducing recurrence from continued mucus extravasation. Incision or aspiration alone commonly recurs because the injured gland remains. Intralesional techniques may be used selectively, but they are less definitive for this recurrent lesion, while full-thickness oncological excision is unnecessarily destructive when no malignant feature is present.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A fit patient undergoes resection and neck dissection for buccal-mucosa squamous-cell carcinoma. Histology shows clear margins but metastatic nodal tumour extends through the capsule into perinodal soft tissue. The head-and-neck board reviews recurrence risk, renal function and hearing before choosing postoperative treatment. Which adjuvant approach is generally indicated for this high-risk feature?
options: Postoperative radiotherapy alone to the primary and nodal basins
Postoperative radiotherapy with concurrent cisplatin when fit
Postoperative surveillance with scheduled clinical and radiological review
correctAnswer: Postoperative radiotherapy with concurrent cisplatin when fit
explanation: Extranodal extension is a major adverse pathological feature in resected oral-cavity squamous carcinoma and generally supports postoperative radiotherapy with concurrent cisplatin in a fit patient. Radiotherapy alone is less appropriate when cisplatin can be given. Cetuximab is not an equivalent routine postoperative substitute for cisplatin, and surveillance alone undertreats this high-risk pathology.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A long-term tobacco chewer has the reported non-healing indurated ulceroproliferative lesion on the buccal mucosa with referred ear pain and a firm cervical node. The head-and-neck team plans deep biopsy and staging imaging before resection and nodal treatment. This feature matters because candidiasis would be scrapable and respond to antifungal therapy. Which diagnosis is most likely?
explanation: A persistent indurated proliferative or ulcerative oral lesion in a tobacco user is squamous-cell carcinoma until biopsy proves otherwise. Depth, anatomic subsite and cervical nodal status determine treatment, while benign inflammatory ulcers should heal after removing the cause.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient has a slow-growing mobile preauricular mass, normal facial movement and Imaging showing a benign tumour confined to the superficial parotid lobe. Cytology is concordant with pleomorphic adenoma. The operation must remove microscopic extensions while avoiding facial-nerve injury. Which procedure is preferred?
options: Simple enucleation through the tumour capsule
Total parotidectomy with routine facial-nerve sacrifice
explanation: A benign superficial-lobe pleomorphic adenoma is commonly treated with superficial parotidectomy or an appropriate formal extracapsular operation that preserves the facial nerve and obtains a safe margin. Simple enucleation risks recurrence from capsule violation and pseudopods.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: During laparoscopic transabdominal preperitoneal hernia repair, the surgeon identifies a hernia sac and must determine whether it is direct or indirect before mesh placement. The reported groin view shows a paired vascular landmark ascending from the external iliac vessels. Additional assessment demonstrates Hesselbach triangle medial to it and the deep ring lateral to it. Which vessels are these?
explanation: The inferior epigastric vessels separate the direct space medially from the indirect deep-ring region laterally and are a key laparoscopic groin landmark. External pudendal, deep circumflex iliac and obturator vessels occupy different planes and do not define direct versus indirect hernias.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with advanced decompensated cirrhosis presents with acute oesophageal-variceal bleeding. Alongside resuscitation, vasoactive therapy and urgent band ligation, short-course antimicrobial prophylaxis is required. Local quinolone resistance is high and the patient recently received a fluoroquinolone, so the team wants dependable gram-negative coverage. In this setting, which regimen is preferred?
explanation: Short-course antibiotic prophylaxis reduces infection, rebleeding and mortality during acute variceal haemorrhage. Intravenous ceftriaxone is preferred in advanced cirrhosis, high quinolone-resistance settings or recent fluoroquinolone exposure. Oral norfloxacin is an alternative where susceptibility is reliable and disease is less advanced, while rifaximin and metronidazole are not equivalent recommended monotherapy for this indication.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: During surgery for a hepatic hydatid cyst, viable protoscolices and pressurised fluid create a risk not present with a simple liver cyst. The operative field is isolated, controlled aspiration and an appropriate scolicidal agent are planned. The anaesthesia team is prepared for anaphylaxis. Which precaution is most important?
options: Inject a scolicidal solution under pressure before isolating the operative field
Open the cyst widely and remove daughter cysts with unprotected suction
Fragment the endocyst and irrigate the peritoneal cavity after evacuation
Isolate the field, aspirate under control and use appropriate scolicidal precautions
correctAnswer: Isolate the field, aspirate under control and use appropriate scolicidal precautions
explanation: Hydatid-fluid spillage can cause anaphylaxis and seed viable scolices, leading to secondary peritoneal echinococcosis and recurrence. The field should therefore be isolated before controlled aspiration and use of an appropriate scolicidal strategy, with the anaesthesia team prepared for anaphylaxis. Pressurised injection, wide unprotected opening and fragmentation increase leakage or dissemination risk.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A shepherd has a slowly enlarging cystic liver lesion and positive Echinococcus serology, but no fever or malignant constitutional symptoms. Ultrasound shows multiple rounded internal cysts within a larger mother cyst. The surgical team plans precautions against spillage and anaphylaxis. Which imaging feature establishes the daughter-cyst pattern?
options: A solitary mural nodule with arterial enhancement
Internal gas with an air-fluid level
explanation: Multiple internal daughter cysts are a characteristic hydatid feature and correspond to a multivesicular Gharbi type III pattern. A simple cyst is homogeneous, a vascular mural nodule raises neoplastic concern and gas suggests infection or communication rather than an uncomplicated daughter-cyst pattern.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A man with cirrhosis and rising alpha-fetoprotein undergoes multiphasic liver CT for a new mass. The reported lesion shows arterial-phase hyperenhancement followed by portal-venous washout and a capsule. The hepatobiliary team assesses portal pressure, liver reserve and transplant or resection eligibility. Which tumour is most likely?
explanation: In a cirrhotic liver, arterial hyperenhancement with venous or delayed washout and capsule is a characteristic imaging pattern of hepatocellular carcinoma. Management then depends on tumour stage, liver function and portal hypertension rather than the image label alone.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 55-year-old patient with fever, right-upper-quadrant pain and sepsis has the reported 8-cm thick-walled hepatic collection. Blood cultures are drawn and broad antibiotics are started, but fever, toxicity and collection size persist. Imaging shows a safe percutaneous route and no surgical perforation requiring laparotomy. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Repeated image-guided needle aspiration without a catheter
Laparoscopic operative drainage of the hepatic collection
Open operative drainage of the hepatic collection
explanation: A large pyogenic liver abscess that remains septic despite antibiotics requires source control. With a safe percutaneous route, image-guided catheter drainage provides continuous evacuation and is preferred for an 8-cm cavity. Repeated needle aspiration may suit smaller or selected collections but often requires multiple procedures. Laparoscopic or open drainage is reserved for percutaneous failure, inaccessible anatomy, rupture or another operative source.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: An 82-year-old man with severe cardiopulmonary disease and limited life expectancy has low-volume, low-grade localised prostate cancer. He wishes to avoid surveillance biopsies and curative-treatment morbidity. The urologist plans symptom-triggered palliative intervention rather than serial testing intended to preserve a later cure option. Which strategy best describes this plan?
explanation: Watchful waiting is conservative symptom-directed care for patients unlikely to benefit from curative treatment because of age, comorbidity or life expectancy. Active surveillance uses scheduled PSA, imaging and sometimes repeat biopsy with curative treatment still intended if risk progresses.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 4-year-old child has haematuria and the reported unilateral renal mass. Imaging and pathology support Wilms tumour without tumour rupture. Multimodal treatment is planned, and staging must include the commonest haematogenous destination because occult disease there changes stage and therapy. In this setting, which site should be specifically screened?
explanation: Wilms tumour most commonly metastasises haematogenously to the lungs, so chest imaging is a routine component of staging. Liver and other sites are also assessed, but pulmonary metastases are the classic priority.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A child has a unilateral renal mass radiologically typical of resectable Wilms tumour without rupture, venous extension or metastasis. The centre follows a primary-nephrectomy protocol, so staging and operative planning proceed while avoiding a manoeuvre that can spill tumour and alter stage. Other international protocols may instead use preoperative chemotherapy. In this setting, which biopsy principle applies here?
options: Image-guided core biopsy followed by neoadjuvant chemotherapy and nephrectomy
Primary nephrectomy without preoperative biopsy under the selected protocol
Open surgical biopsy followed by delayed definitive nephrectomy
Imaging-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy without biopsy under a SIOP protocol
correctAnswer: Primary nephrectomy without preoperative biopsy under the selected protocol
explanation: Under protocols using upfront nephrectomy for a typical resectable Wilms tumour, preoperative biopsy is avoided because spill or biopsy can upstage disease. Management must follow the selected cooperative-group protocol: SIOP pathways often use imaging-based preoperative chemotherapy, whereas atypical or unresectable masses may require a different tissue strategy.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A young man with chronic pilonidal pits presents with an acutely painful fluctuant natal-cleft swelling and fever. There is no extensive spreading necrosis. The surgeon separates immediate abscess source control from later elective treatment of persistent sinus tracts after inflammation settles. What is the initial management?
options: Wide excision with complex flap closure during acute sepsis
Incision and drainage of the abscess
Oral antibiotics alone despite a fluctuant collection
correctAnswer: Incision and drainage of the abscess
explanation: An acute pilonidal abscess requires incision and drainage. Antibiotics are added for cellulitis or systemic risk, while definitive pit excision or off-midline flap treatment is considered later for recurrent or chronic disease rather than during uncontrolled acute inflammation.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with diabetes presents with fever, severe constant anal pain and the reported tender erythematous perianal swelling. There is fluctuance without a thrombosed blue nodule, and pelvic sepsis is considered while glycaemia and antibiotic indications are assessed. A fistula search is deferred until acute drainage. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Perform needle aspiration with culture-directed antimicrobial coverage
Begin antimicrobial coverage and reassess for drainage after 48 hours
Perform drainage with primary fistulotomy before defining tract anatomy
Perform prompt incision and drainage with indicated antimicrobial coverage
correctAnswer: Perform prompt incision and drainage with indicated antimicrobial coverage
explanation: An anorectal abscess requires prompt incision and drainage because aspiration or antibiotics do not reliably empty an established cavity. Diabetes and systemic features support adjunctive antimicrobial therapy, but antibiotics must not delay source control. Primary fistulotomy is reserved for a clearly defined simple tract when sphincter risk is acceptable; it should not be performed blindly during acute drainage.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A young man has recurrent painful swelling and purulent discharge from pits in the midline natal cleft, worse after prolonged sitting. The reported lesion is separate from the anal verge. Examination finds no perianal internal opening or multiple axillary-groin lesions suggestive of another sinus disorder. Which diagnosis is most likely?
explanation: Pilonidal disease produces midline natal-cleft pits, hair-containing sinuses and recurrent abscess or discharge. A fistula-in-ano relates to the anal canal, hidradenitis is usually multifocal in apocrine-bearing skin and an epidermoid cyst lacks characteristic midline pits.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: After difficult laparoscopic cholecystectomy, a patient develops abdominal sepsis, bilious drain output and jaundice. The operating surgeon suspects a major bile-duct injury, but immediate blind repair could worsen tissue loss. Resuscitation, drainage and broad antibiotics are started while cholangiographic anatomy is defined. What is the safest management principle?
options: Perform immediate end-to-end repair before defining proximal anatomy
Control sepsis, define the injury and refer early to HPB expertise
Use ERCP stenting before determining whether the duct is transected
Maintain external drainage and refer after a mature stricture develops
correctAnswer: Control sepsis, define the injury and refer early to HPB expertise
explanation: Major bile-duct injury requires source control, sepsis management, precise delineation of biliary anatomy and early involvement of an experienced hepatopancreatobiliary team. Immediate repair before defining anatomy risks further injury, and ERCP cannot bridge a complete transection. Delaying referral until a mature stricture forms loses the benefit of early specialist planning. Definitive repair is timed and performed under expert conditions because failed local attempts increase stricture and vascular-complication risk.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A patient with fever, jaundice and right-upper-quadrant pain becomes hypotensive and confused from severe acute cholangitis. Fluids, blood cultures and antibiotics are started, and ultrasound shows a dilated bile duct with an obstructing stone. Source control cannot wait for elective gallbladder surgery. In this setting, which procedure is preferred when available?
options: Perform urgent percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage
Perform urgent endoscopic ultrasound-guided biliary drainage
Perform urgent ERCP with biliary drainage
Perform urgent open common-bile-duct exploration and drainage
correctAnswer: Perform urgent ERCP with biliary drainage
explanation: Severe acute cholangitis requires urgent biliary decompression after parallel resuscitation and antibiotics. ERCP is preferred when papillary access is available because it provides internal drainage and stone therapy with less morbidity than operative exploration. Percutaneous or endoscopic-ultrasound drainage is used when ERCP fails or is anatomically impossible, while open exploration is reserved for situations in which less invasive drainage cannot be achieved.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: A 46-year-old woman presents with biliary colic, jaundice and cholestatic liver-test elevation after ultrasound demonstrates gallbladder stones and a dilated common bile duct. ERCP shows a discrete intraluminal filling defect near the distal duct. The endoscopist plans sphincterotomy if the lesion is removable. What is the most likely finding?
explanation: A discrete filling defect in a dilated common bile duct with biliary colic and gallstones indicates choledocholithiasis, and ERCP can provide extraction after sphincterotomy. A stricture or tumour produces narrowing rather than a mobile filling defect, while sclerosing cholangitis causes multifocal stricturing and beading.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Surgery
question: After cholecystectomy, a patient has persistent bile drainage but no jaundice. Cholangiography shows an aberrant right sectoral duct has been transected and clipped proximally, with bile leaking from the isolated distal segment and no continuity to the common bile duct. Expert biliary reconstruction is considered. In this setting, which Strasberg category is this?
